concepts.name: The $14 Reality Check
concepts.angle: Calls out the absurdity of $997 courses teaching people to sell digital products, while Kaizen gives you 400,000 of them for $14. Price-contrast positioning against the overpriced MRR/PLR market.
concepts.key_message: Everyone else charges $997 to teach you what this $14 gives you outright.
concepts.research_support: MRR/PLR competitors charge $497–$2,000. The $14 price point is a category-disrupting weapon when contrasted directly.
concepts.differentiation: No competitor in this space runs price-contrast ads — they all justify their high price. This flips the script.
concepts.example_hook: They charged $997 to teach you how to sell digital products. I'm charging $14 to give you 400,000 of them.

concepts.name: The Inventory Problem
concepts.angle: Agitates the #1 bottleneck every aspiring digital entrepreneur faces — not having a product to sell — then positions Kaizen as the direct, immediate solution.
concepts.key_message: The only thing stopping your digital business is not having a product. Here are 400,000.
concepts.research_support: 'I don't have anything to sell' is the #1 objection to starting a digital product business. This meets buyers at that exact stuck moment.
concepts.differentiation: Competitors talk about freedom and income. This goes one level deeper to the specific bottleneck buyers are stuck at.
concepts.example_hook: You've been watching other people sell digital products. You know why you haven't started. You don't have a product. Here are 400,000.

concepts.name: The $14 Math
concepts.angle: Visual-first concept showing the pure logic: sell 1 product, make your $14 back, everything else is profit. No income claims — just undeniable math anyone processes in 3 seconds.
concepts.key_message: One sale covers your entire investment. The other 399,999 products are pure upside.
concepts.research_support: The $14 price is so low the math becomes the proof. No testimonials needed — the numbers do the selling.
concepts.differentiation: No one in this market runs math-based ads. Everyone runs lifestyle. This stands out visually and logically.
concepts.example_hook: $14 in. Sell 1 product for $14. You're already even. What do you do with the other 399,999?

concepts.name: The Already Entrepreneur
concepts.angle: Targets perpetual researchers — people who already identify as entrepreneurs, follow business content, save income posts, but haven't launched. Validates their identity and delivers the final nudge.
concepts.key_message: You've been thinking about it long enough. Here's what you've been waiting for.
concepts.research_support: A large segment of the make-money-online audience is in perpetual research mode. Identity-based messaging converts this warmer audience better than benefit-focused ads.
concepts.differentiation: Competitors target 'anyone who wants to make money.' This targets people who already self-identify as entrepreneurs — higher intent, closer to buying.
concepts.example_hook: You've saved 47 posts about making money online. You've watched the videos. You know it's possible. So what are you waiting for?

concepts.name: The Wall of Products
concepts.angle: Zero copy. Just a dense overwhelming grid of product mockups filling the entire frame, then one line: 400,000 products. $14. Master Resell Rights. Pure visual volume creates awe and disbelief.
concepts.key_message: The sheer volume at this price is impossible to ignore.
concepts.research_support: The product grid on the landing page already converts. Bringing that visual weight into the ad feed creates a scroll-stopping experience competitors aren't using.
concepts.differentiation: Every competitor uses lifestyle imagery — beaches, laptops, cash. A pure product-grid ad is visually alien in the feed and stops scrolls.
